name: Thorium
name_origin: Named after Thor, the Norse god of thunder, by Jons Jakob Berzelius.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Approximately 9.6 ppm in Earth's crust, about three times more abundant than uranium. Principal mineral is monazite, a phosphate mineral; also found in thorite and thorianite.

compounds.name: Thorium dioxide (thoria)
compounds.summary: Extremely refractory white solid with one of the highest melting points of any oxide (3390 degrees C); historically used in gas-mantle lighting (Welsbach mantles) and high-temperature ceramics.

compounds.name: Thorium tetrafluoride
compounds.summary: Used as an anti-reflection coating in high-quality optical components for ultraviolet and infrared wavelengths.

compounds.name: Thorium nitrate
compounds.summary: Water-soluble salt used historically to impregnate gas mantles and as a starting material for other thorium compounds.

compounds.name: Thorium tetrachloride
compounds.summary: Hygroscopic white solid; intermediate in metallic thorium production via reduction with calcium or sodium.

uses: Proposed nuclear fuel in thorium-fueled reactors via the Th-232 to U-233 breeding cycle
Alloying agent in magnesium for aerospace applications, increasing strength at high temperatures
High-quality lenses and optical instruments due to its high refractive index (now mostly replaced)
Tungsten welding electrodes (thoriated tungsten) for arc welding
Historically used in gas mantles for lanterns
Geological dating via the uranium-thorium method
reactivity_summary: A silvery white metal that tarnishes to gray and then black in air. Powdered thorium is pyrophoric and can ignite spontaneously. It is slowly attacked by water and dissolves in concentrated hydrochloric acid; chemistry is dominated by the +4 oxidation state.
isotopes_summary: All isotopes are radioactive. Thorium-232 (half-life 14.05 billion years) accounts for nearly all natural thorium and is the parent of the thorium decay series. Thorium-229 and thorium-230 are also of scientific interest.

citations.source: IUPAC Periodic Table of the Elements
CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd ed.
